attributes.stem:
A seven-year-old boy presents to the Emergency Department with a two-day history of vomiting and confusion. He was treated with aspirin last week for influenza-like symptoms. He has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications.

On examination, he appears drowsy with a pulse rate of 90/min, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. He is oriented only to person and has asterixis. His liver edge is palpable 4 cm below the costal margin. Blood tests show ALT 350 U/L (5-30), AST 400 U/L (5-40), and serum ammonia 150 µmol/L (11-35).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following best explains the underlying pathophysiology?
options: Mitochondrial dysfunction leading to impaired fatty acid oxidation
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with the classic features of Reye syndrome: acute encephalopathy and hepatic dysfunction following a viral illness treated with aspirin. The condition predominantly affects children and can be life-threatening if not recognised early.

Mitochondrial dysfunction leading to impaired fatty acid oxidation is the primary pathophysiological mechanism. This results in accumulation of fatty acids within hepatocytes and impaired energy metabolism, leading to cellular dysfunction. The exact trigger for mitochondrial injury remains unclear but appears related to the interaction between aspirin and viral infection.

Autoimmune destruction of hepatocytes typically presents more insidiously with chronic liver inflammation, elevated immunoglobulins, and autoantibodies. The acute presentation and recent viral illness make this mechanism unlikely.

Direct viral hepatotoxicity causes a different pattern of liver injury. While viruses like hepatitis A can cause acute hepatitis in children, the association with aspirin use and the presence of encephalopathy point away from this mechanism.

Endotoxin-mediated hepatocellular injury occurs in conditions like sepsis where bacterial products trigger systemic inflammation. This patient lacks features of infection such as fever or elevated inflammatory markers.

Inherited urea cycle enzyme deficiency typically presents in infancy with recurrent episodes of hyperammonemia and encephalopathy. While the presentation can be similar, these disorders are not associated with aspirin use or viral illnesses.
learningPoints: Reye syndrome is characterised by acute hepatic dysfunction and encephalopathy, typically following viral illness treated with aspirin in children.
Mitochondrial dysfunction is the key pathophysiological mechanism in Reye syndrome, leading to impaired fatty acid metabolism.
Clinical features include vomiting, confusion, hepatomegaly and elevated liver enzymes with hyperammonemia.
references: The Mechanism for Aspirin or Salicylates Induced Reye's Syndrome in Children with Viral Infections. In: Evidence Based Medicine Consult [online]. 2018. Available at: https://www.ebmconsult.com/articles/reye-syndrome-asa-aspirin-mechanism-liver-encephalopathy
Schrör K. Aspirin and Reye syndrome: a review of the evidence. Paediatr Drugs. 2007;9(3):195-204. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17523700/

attributes.name: Graduating
